content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcnr- PRACTTcEs CorrarrarssroN
428 J Street o Suire 620 o Sacramento. CA g5gl4_232g
(916\ 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
April 1,2009
Frank L. Mecham
Supervisor, First District
1055 Monterey, Room D430
San Luis Obispo, CA 93408-1003
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our file No.I-09-026
Dear Mr. Mecham:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This l"tterls based solely on the
facts presented to us in your request. The Fair Political Practices Commissior,l..tfr.
Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Ogtesby
(1975) I FPPC ops.7l.) Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance.r
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, oi other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest.
QUESTION
Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from participating in
decisions by the San Luis obispo County Board of Supervisors on litigation against the
Board involving property located near property in which you have a 25 percen"t
ownership interest?
I The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections g1000 through gl0l4. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
PoliticalPractices Commission are contained in sections l8l l0 through 1g997 of ritle 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory retbrences are to Title 2, Division-6 of the California Code of
Regulaticlns. unless otherwise indicated.
I Inftrrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 l4; Regulation tSi:StctiJl. c.py enclosed-)
..;'


File No. l-09-026
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Based on the facts as you describe them, it is reasonably foreseeable that
decisions regarding the litigation would have a material financial effect on your property.
Therefore, you have a disqualifying conflict of interest.
FACTS
ln 2008, you inherited a25 percent interest in property located in the town of
Santa Margarita, worth at least $2,000. As of Janu ari 6,ioob,you serve on the San Luis
Obispo County Board of Supervisors (the ..Board,,).
Before you took your position on the Board, the Board approved Tentative Tract
Map 2586 and Conditional Use Permit for the Santa Margarita [.anch, LLC. Two
environmental non-profit groups filed suit against the Cointy of San Luis Obispo
challenging the Board's approval of Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC,s residentiar
development plan. This plan involves buildingl residential tract subdivision on a section
9f the Santa Margarita Ranch, a 14,000 acre plot of land within San Luis obispo County.
The area where this residential subdivision would be built is about a mile or more from
your property. In our telephone conversations, you explained that the residential
subdivision is part of a larger scheme of development ior Santa Margarita Ranch, and
that after this portion of the development movei forward, other areas-are slated for
development as well. If this subdivision is built out, there are plans to continue building
on the 14,000 acres.
During our telephone conversations, you stated that the pending law suit
challenges the Board's -aqproval of a plan that would allow Sania Mariarita Ranch, LLC
to build a residential subdivision in Santa Margarita, about a mile o, -o." from your
property. It is your opinion that the outcome of this law suit will have a direct blaring on
the development of the rest of the phases plarured for the Santa Margarita Ranch. you
also stated that the phases of development are interrelated and the out"o-" of the
residential subdivision could directly affect those areas of development that ar; within
500 feet of your property.
The Act's conflict-of-inter"rr r$,t'"It"Trure thar pubric officiars ..perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them.', (Section gl00l(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" u!or.**"rtut
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
The Commis_1ion has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis to decide whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation lg70b@Xl)_(g).) The
general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest.iirtr whenever a public official makes
a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests. (Section g7103.)


File No. I-09-026
Page No. 3
Step One: Are you a public Official?
As a member of the San Luis Obispo County Board of Supervisors, you are a
public official under the Act. (section g2o4g.) consequently, you .uy ,oi make,
participate in making, or otherwise use your official position io innu.r"e any decisions
that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your 
""onomic
interests. (Regulations 18702. l- I 8702.4.)
step Two: will yo-u be making, participating in making, or using or
attempting to use your oflicial position to influenc* go"."nmental decision?
You have stated that-you expec! that_decisions regarding a lawsuit challenging the
subdivision development will come before the Board. FJr purposes of this analysis, we
assume that you will have decisions before you related to the lawsuit; the analysis that
follows applies to those potential decisions.
Step Three: Do you have a financial interest in the decisions at issue?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulations 18703-lSiol.s.) The applicable economic
interests include:
1. An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section g7103(a),
Regulation 18703.1(a).) An interest in any business entity in
which a public official is a director, officei, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section
87 103(d), Regulation I 8703. l(b).)
2. An interest in real property in which a public official has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 o, moi". (Section g7103(b),
Regulation 18703.2.)
3. Any source of income, including promised income, to the
public official that aggregates to $50b or more within 12 months
prior to the decision. (section g7103(c), Regulation 1g703.3.)
4. Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within l2 months prior to the decision. tsectiJn
87 103(e), Regularion lg7 03.4.)
5. A public official also has an economic interest in his or her
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of
his or her immediate family. This is also known as the .,personal
financial effects" mle. (Section g7103, Regulation tszd:.s.1


File No. l-09-026
Page No.4
Based on the facts you provided, you have an interest in the property within the
town of Santa Margarita in which you have a 25 percent ownership int"i.rt. you have
stated that this interest is at least $2,000. You have not provided information regarding
any other economic interests that might bear on this analysis; we therefore only iaar"rt
the Santa Margarita property.
Step Four: Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if any part of the real property is within 500 feet of
the boundaries of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision.
(Regulation 18704.2(a)(l).) If the real property is not directly involved, it is indirectly
involved. (Regulation 1870a(a).)
_ 
Your property is greater than 500 feet away from the property that is the subject
of the law suit about which the Board will be making decisions. ^fne ect presumes that
the property is indirectly involved.
Step Five: Materiality Standard
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. lRegllation
18700(a).) If the real property in which a public official has an economic interest is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standard in Regulation
18705.2(b) applies. That is, because your property is more than 500 feet frJm the
property that is the subject of the govemmental decisions, the effects of the decisions are
presumed not to be material. The presumption can be rebutted with proof that the
circumstances suggest that it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decisions
will have a material financial effect on the property. (Regulation 18705.2(b).) A partial
list of circumstances you would consider when determining materiality include:
(A) The development potential or income producing potential
of the real property in which the official has an ecbnomic intirest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy,lntensity of
use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborirood.
(Regulation I 8702.5(b)(A)-(C).)
The project that is the subject of the lawsuit about which the Board will be
making decisions is a residential subdivision about a mile or more from your property.
You have not provided facts that could rebut the presumption of materiaiity, but given ttre


File No. I-09-026
Page No. 5
factors above, among others that might apply to your circumstances, you can determine
whether there are any factors affecting your property that would U" *ut..iuLl-
Step Six: Is it Reasonabty Foreseeable that the Decision Will Have a
Material Financial Impact on your property interest?
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 1S706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. l9g.)
During our telephone conversations, you stated that the pending law suit
challenges the Board's -aqgroval of a plan thit would allow Sania Mariarita Ranch, LLC
to build a residential subdivision in Santa Margarita, about a mile or rnor" from your
property. It is your opinion that the outcome of this law suit will have a direct bearing on
the development of the rest 9f the phases planned for the Santa Margarita Ranch. you
also-stated that the phases of development are intenelated and the out.o*" of the
residential subdivision could directly affect those areas of development that are within
500 feet of your property. Given this information, your opinion seems to be that it is
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decisions the Board will be making
regarding the pending lawsuit will have a material financial effect on your prof".ty i,
Santa Margarita.
Step 7: Does the,.public Generally', Exception Apply?
Even when there is a disqualifying conflict of interest, the "public generally,,
exception sometimes.applies to allow participation by a public official who is otherwise
unable to participate in govemmental decisions.
*Notwithstanding a determination that the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a pubiic official's
economic interests is material, a public official does not have a
disqualifying conflict of interest in the governmental decision if
the official can establish that the governmental decision will affect
the public official's economic interests in a manner [that] is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect
the public generally as set forth in Title2 california code of
Regulations sections 18707.1 - rg7o7.g j' (Regulation 1g707.)
we have previously advised that the mere existence of additional factors is not sufficient to rebut
the presumption of non-materiality. The presumption that an indirectly involved p.op.ny *iii not oe
materially affected is rebutted only if the governmental decision will have a clearly distinguishable and
substantial financial effect on an official's property. (See Matranga Advice Letter, A-07-o64: Berger
Advice Letter, 4-05-054.)


File No. I-09-026
Page No. 6
If the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of a governmental decision
on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable "from its effect on the
public generally," then the public official does not h-ave a conflict. (Section g7103;
Regulations I 8700(b) (7), 187 07 (a).)
To come under the purview of the public generally exception, the decision
affecting the public official's economic interests must alsl affect a significant segment of
the public generally_ in substantially the same manner as it affects the"public o6i.iut.
(Regulation 18707.1(b)(l) and (2).) The public generally exceprion applies if both
prongs of this two-prong test are met. Firit the oificial must determine if the decision
affects a significant segment of the population. If the prong is met, then the official must
determine that the segment is affectidln substantiatly ttre ,u*, *u*er as the public
official.
For real property, the "significant segment" test is met if the decision in question
also affects ten percent or lore of all property owners in the jurisdiction of the official,s
agency or the district the official represents, to qualify under the public g.n.ruity
exception' (Regulation 18707.1(b)(B)(i).) This-exception also aiplies iithe decision
{ryq 51000 property owners in the official's jurisdiction or distiict. (Regulation
18707.t(bxl)(BXii).)
Because we do not have facts to determine whether this exception would apply in
your-situation' we provide this information.for you to base your decision regarding
whether the public generally exception applies.4
Step Eight: Does the Legally Required Exception Apply?
The facts as you describe them do not suggest that this exception would apply; we
therefore do no analyze this step.
If you have other questions on this matter, please conract me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Lbdl* l-hla,-
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
HMRjgl
Enclosure
a Infbrmation provided on the San Luis obispo website seems to.indicate that the subject property
is not located in your district. This would make ir highly untitelt, ;i;;, the known facts. that?e public
generally exception would apply.

formal written advice. (Section 831 l4; Regulation tSi:StctiJl. c.py enclosed-)
..;'


File No. l-09-026

CONCLUSION: Based on the facts as you describe them, it is reasonably foreseeable that
